teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the confusion students usually feel when the same point can be described in different frames.
teaching_arc.progression: Build intuition first, then connect frames to Fanuc programming practice.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish able to explain and use user frames as a practical programming tool.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Make frames feel intuitive so students can understand and use user frames confidently in Fanuc programming.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand that a frame changes how motion is described, not where the robot physically is.
Students should distinguish world, tool, and user frames.
Students should be able to explain why user frames simplify programming relative to part geometry.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Use moving-object examples to make frame attachment intuitive.
Pause often to separate physical motion from coordinate description.
transition_style: Move from the industrial question students would ask on the job to the next specification, control, or programming choice they need to make.

slide_ref.source_title: Frames, Motion, and FANUC User Frames

slides.narration_seed: 2D first. Then connect to FANUC user frames and Lab 5.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Make frames feel intuitive so students can understand and use user frames confidently in Fanuc programming.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Session Roadmap so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Frames, Motion, and FANUC User Frames as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Frames, Motion, and FANUC User Frames connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Frames, Motion, and FANUC User Frames sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Use Session Roadmap to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: 1) Frames (origin + axes)
2) 2D translation and rotation
3) Transform formulas (forward + inverse)
4) Robot frames: Base / Tool / User
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Frames, Motion, and FANUC User Frames into Session Roadmap so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What is a Frame? (start in 2D) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Use Session Roadmap to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Session Roadmap connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Session Roadmap sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for What is a Frame? (start in 2D) using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: A frame is a local coordinate system
It has an origin and axes (X, Y in 2D)
A point like (2,1) only has meaning in a frame
Robotics uses many frames at once (base, tool, user…)
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quiz: frame meaning so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for What is a Frame? (start in 2D) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep What is a Frame? (start in 2D) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture What is a Frame? (start in 2D) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: frame meaning into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: You wrote a point as (200, 50). What is missing to make this meaningful?
Coordinates must be expressed in a specific frame (World, User, Tool, etc.).
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: frame meaning and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: frame meaning into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: frame meaning question on their own and explain the reasoning.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Define In 3D we add Z (right-handed) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: 2D idea stays the same; 3D just adds Z
Right-hand rule: curl +X to +Y, thumb points +Z
In FANUC: frames are right-handed (X,Y,Z)
We won’t do heavy 3D derivations today — focus on correct usage
slides.transition_from_previous: Use In 3D we add Z (right-handed) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once In 3D we add Z (right-handed) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.story_hint: Keep In 3D we add Z (right-handed) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain In 3D we add Z (right-handed) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Main frames you must know using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Base frame: fixed to robot base
Tool frame (TCP): attached to tool tip
User frame: attached to table/fixture/part
Same physical point → different numbers in each frame
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Why frames matter (technician reality) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Main frames you must know first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Main frames you must know connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Main frames you must know clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Define Why frames matter (technician reality) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Wrong frame = robot moves in the wrong direction
Wrong TCP = correct direction but wrong path/height
Wrong user frame = points look shifted/tilted on the part
Best habit: always check active UFRAME/UTOOL before teaching points
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Why frames matter (technician reality) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Why frames matter (technician reality) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Define Why frames matter (technician reality) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Why frames matter (technician reality) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Why frames matter (technician reality) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slide_ref.source_title: Quiz: identify the most likely mistake

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: identify the most likely mistake into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: Robot follows the correct XY shape, but the entire path is consistently 5 mm too high everywhere. Most likely issue?
A constant Z offset everywhere is typically TCP/UTOOL or height reference, not a user-frame axis direction.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: identify the most likely mistake and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: identify the most likely mistake without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: identify the most likely mistake into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: identify the most likely mistake question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slide_ref.source_title: World vs User: same point, different coordinates

slides.narration_seed: Compare the alternatives or concepts on World vs User: same point, different coordinates clearly, emphasize the trade-off, and end with the decision rule students should remember.
must_cover: A point can have different numbers in different frames
World might show (350,120,0)
User might show (50,20,0)
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Same physical point — new reference
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Quiz: identify the most likely mistake into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on World vs User: same point, different coordinates explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of World vs User: same point, different coordinates and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slide_ref.source_title: Pure translation (2D): shift origin, keep axes parallel

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Pure translation (2D): shift origin, keep axes parallel using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Translation = sliding without turning
Axes stay parallel (same orientation)
Only the origin changes: (t=[t_x,t_y]^T)
This matches a rigid fixture shift (no rotation)
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Translation in 2D (exact) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Pure translation (2D): shift origin, keep axes parallel first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Pure translation (2D): shift origin, keep axes parallel connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Pure translation (2D): shift origin, keep axes parallel clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Define Translation in 2D (exact) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: $$p_w=\begin{bmatrix}x_w\\y_w\end{bmatrix},\quad p_u=\begin{bmatrix}x_u\\y_u\end{bmatrix},\quad t=\begin{bmatrix}t_x\\t_y\end{bmatrix}$$
Meaning: add the new frame’s origin shift to get world coordinates.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Translation in 2D (exact) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Translation in 2D (exact) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Translation in 2D (exact) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Translation in 2D (exact) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Translation in 2D (exact) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: fixture shifted into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: A fixture moves +10 mm in User X and +0 mm in User Y (no rotation). Your points were taught in that user frame. Best action?
A rigid shift is exactly what user frames are for: update the frame (origin), not each point.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: fixture shifted and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: fixture shifted without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: fixture shifted into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: fixture shifted question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slide_ref.source_title: Pure rotation (2D): same origin, axes turn by θ

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Pure rotation (2D): same origin, axes turn by θ using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Rotation = turning without sliding
Origin stays fixed
Axes directions change by angle (\theta)
This matches a fixture that is rotated on the table
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Rotation matrix in 2D (exact) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Pure rotation (2D): same origin, axes turn by θ first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Pure rotation (2D): same origin, axes turn by θ connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Pure rotation (2D): same origin, axes turn by θ clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Define Rotation matrix in 2D (exact) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: $$R(\theta)=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$$
$$p_w = R(\theta)\,p_u$$
Interpretation: multiplying by (R(\theta)) rotates coordinates.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Rotation matrix in 2D (exact) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Rotation matrix in 2D (exact) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Rotation matrix in 2D (exact) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Rotation matrix in 2D (exact) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Rotation matrix in 2D (exact) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: wrong rotation direction into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: You rotate a frame +90° about Z, but your result looks like −90° (everything turns the opposite way). Most likely mistake?
For rotations, the inverse is the transpose. Using the inverse flips the direction of rotation.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: wrong rotation direction and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: wrong rotation direction without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: wrong rotation direction into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: wrong rotation direction question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Rotation + translation together (real setups) using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Most setups have both: rotate then translate
This is the standard frame conversion used in controllers
We call this a rigid transform
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Forward + inverse formulas (memorize these) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Rotation + translation together (real setups) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Rotation + translation together (real setups) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Rotation + translation together (real setups) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Define Forward + inverse formulas (memorize these) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: $$\textbf{Forward:}\quad p_w = R\,p_u + t$$
$$\textbf{Inverse:}\quad p_u = R^T\,(p_w - t)$$
Key fact: (R^{-1}=R^T) for rotation matrices (orthonormal).
This is why “undoing” a rotation is easy and stable.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Forward + inverse formulas (memorize these) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Forward + inverse formulas (memorize these) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Forward + inverse formulas (memorize these) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Forward + inverse formulas (memorize these) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Forward + inverse formulas (memorize these) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: Y axis is flipped (mirrored) into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: You taught a user frame. Jog +X is correct, but jog +Y goes opposite (mirrored). Most likely cause?
Point 3 defines which side of the X axis is +Y (the plane direction). Wrong side flips Y.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: Y axis is flipped (mirrored) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: Y axis is flipped (mirrored) without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: Y axis is flipped (mirrored) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: Y axis is flipped (mirrored) question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Define 3D uses the same idea (homogeneous transform) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: $$T=\begin{bmatrix}R & t\\0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$$
$$\begin{bmatrix}p_w\\1\end{bmatrix}=T\,\begin{bmatrix}p_u\\1\end{bmatrix}$$
$$T^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}R^T & -R^T t\\0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$$
User frames fix rigid motion (translation + rotation), not scaling/warping.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use 3D uses the same idea (homogeneous transform) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once 3D uses the same idea (homogeneous transform) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define 3D uses the same idea (homogeneous transform) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep 3D uses the same idea (homogeneous transform) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain 3D uses the same idea (homogeneous transform) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Teach 2D Transform Playground (live numbers) through the visual or interactive material, narrating what students should notice, why it matters, and how it connects to the topic goal.
must_cover: Drag User origin (square) → translation (t)
Drag rotation handle (circle) → angle (\theta)
Drag point (dot) → changes (p_u)
Tell students exactly what to watch for in the visual or interactive material.
Observe live:
slides.transition_from_previous: Shift from explanation into a visual or interactive view of 2D Transform Playground (live numbers) so students can watch the concept, not just hear it.
slides.transition_to_next: After the visual explanation, step back out and explain the decision rule or takeaway students should keep.
slides.explanation_style: Teach through the visual or interactive material on 2D Transform Playground (live numbers) by narrating what students should notice and why it matters.
slides.story_hint: Keep 2D Transform Playground (live numbers) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand what to watch for on 2D Transform Playground (live numbers) and why that visible behavior matters technically.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: FK vs IK (concept only — why frames matter)

slides.narration_seed: Compare the alternatives or concepts on FK vs IK (concept only — why frames matter) clearly, emphasize the trade-off, and end with the decision rule students should remember.
must_cover: FK: joints → tool pose (where is tool now?)
IK: desired pose → joints (how to reach it?)
Controller uses frames/TCP to define the pose
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Wrong UFRAME/UTOOL = controller solves the wrong target
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from 2D Transform Playground (live numbers) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on FK vs IK (concept only — why frames matter) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of FK vs IK (concept only — why frames matter) and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slide_ref.source_title: FANUC User Frame = a frame attached to the job

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for FANUC User Frame = a frame attached to the job using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: User frame matches the fixture/part orientation
Teach points relative to the job, not the robot base
If fixture moves rigidly, update the frame → points still work
This is why user frames save hours of reteaching
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quiz: weld line on 30° angled surface so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for FANUC User Frame = a frame attached to the job first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep FANUC User Frame = a frame attached to the job connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture FANUC User Frame = a frame attached to the job clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slide_ref.source_title: Quiz: weld line on 30° angled surface

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: weld line on 30° angled surface into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: You must weld a straight line on a surface tilted 30° relative to robot base. Best strategy?
Aligning the user frame to the part makes straight motion and orientation intuitive; controller handles transforms.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slide_ref.source_title: What a User Frame can and cannot fix

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for What a User Frame can and cannot fix using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: User frame fixes rigid motion only
Rigid = translation + rotation
Not rigid = scale/warp/bad TCP/geometry change
If the part is larger (scale) → frame won’t fix it
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quiz: part scaled (not rigid) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for What a User Frame can and cannot fix first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep What a User Frame can and cannot fix connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture What a User Frame can and cannot fix clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a moving base, car, or robot frame on screen so the attached coordinate frame visibly moves with the object.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: part scaled (not rigid) into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: A new batch of parts is ~2% larger (scaled). Can you fix it by updating only the User Frame?
Scaling changes distances between features. User frames only correct rigid shifts/rotations.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slide_ref.source_title: Three-Point method: what the 3 points mean

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Three-Point method: what the 3 points mean using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: P1: Origin (new zero)
P2: defines +X direction (P1 → P2)
P3: defines which side is +Y (sets plane orientation)
Rule: spread points out for better accuracy
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quiz: +X jog moves diagonally so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: +X jog moves diagonally into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: In User Frame jogging, +X should move along an edge, but it moves diagonally. Best fix?
Point 2 defines the X axis direction. If it’s off (or too close/noisy), +X becomes rotated/diagonal.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for After teaching: fastest verification using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Jog +X: should follow template X direction
Jog +Y: should follow template Y direction
Jog +Z: should go up (safe height)
If wrong: re-teach the frame points before running

must_cover: MENU → SETUP → FRAMES → USER (UFRAME)
Select frame number (ex: 6)
METHOD → Three Point
Record: Origin → X point → XY-plane point

slides.narration_seed: Define Program habit (must-do every time) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: At start of program set frames explicitly:
Reason: if operator changed active frame, your program still runs correctly
If you forget this, robot can execute points in the wrong frame

slides.narration_seed: Use Fast troubleshooting checklist (shop flow) as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: 1) Confirm active UFRAME_NUM and UTOOL_NUM
2) Jog +X/+Y/+Z in user frame (does it match the part?)
3) If X wrong → re-teach Point 2
Students should understand that a frame changes how motion is described, not where the robot physically is.
4) If Y flipped → re-teach Point 3 (wrong side)

slides.narration_seed: Use Core takeaway (remember forever) as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: Frames define meaning of coordinates
User frames make programs reusable when fixtures move rigidly
Math: (p_w = R p_u + t) and (p_u = R^T(p_w - t))
Students should understand that a frame changes how motion is described, not where the robot physically is.
Rotation inverse is easy: (R^{-1}=R^T)
